content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 3, 1990


Honorable Tom Clark
Councilman, 4th District
Office of the City Council
Civic Center Plaza
333 West Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach, CA 90802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-432

Dear Mr. Clark:

Thank you for your request for advice regarding the transfer
provision of the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTION

Is it permissible for you to transfer funds solicited in the
past or future under the auspices of the "Tom Clark for City
Council" controlled committee to the "Clark for Mayor Committee"
for the purpose of retiring the deficit remaining from your
mayoral campaign?

CONCLUSION

You may transfer funds at any time from your council commit-
tee to your mayoral committee. You may not transfer funds from
the mayoral committee to the council committee after the post-
election reporting period for the mayoral contest because those
then become "surplus funds" which are subject to the strictures of
Section 85807.

FACTS

You are an incumbent member of the Long Beach City Council.
In your most recent council campaign, your controlled committee

_____________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. A-90-432
Page 2


was the "Tom Clark for City Council" committee, which remains
extant. This year you campaigned unsuccessfully for the office of
mayor; your controlled committee in the mayoral contest was the
"Clark for Mayor" committee, which has a current deficit. You
would like to retire the deficit from the mayoral funds with funds
from the "Tom Clark for City Council" committee.

ANALYSIS

The Act, as amended by Proposition 73, prohibits transfers of
funds between controlled committees. However, on May 15, 1989,
the United States District Court, Eastern District of California
enjoined the Commission from enforcing that portion of Section
85304 which prohibited a candidate or elected officer from
transferring funds among his or her own controlled committees.
(Service Employees International Union, AFLO-CIO, et al. v. Fair
Political Practices Commission (Case No. CIVS-89-0433 LKK-JPM
("SEIU")).) Therefore, you may transfer funds from your city
council committee to the "Clark for Mayor Committee."

Regarding the above, there is one important caveat. Section
85807, which deals with distribution of surplus campaign funds
remaining at the conclusion of the post-election reporting period
following the defeat of a candidate, does not allow a transfer of
those surplus funds to a committee which supports or opposes a
candidate for elective office. What that means is that you may
transfer council committee funds to the mayoral committee account,
but you may not transfer mayoral committee funds to the council
committee account once those funds have become "surplus funds."
(See Leece Advice Letter, No. I-90-061, copy enclosed.)

The injunction handed down in the SEIU case is a preliminary
injunction; the Commission is awaiting a final ruling from the
court, which may or may not deem the challenged sections of
Proposition 73, including the transfer provision,
unconstitutional. Until the court makes its ruling, however, our
advice is that a transfer such as the one you contemplate, from
your council committee to your mayoral committee is permitted.
(See Maron Advice Letter, No. I-90-202, copy enclosed.)

Although a transfer of funds to pay debts of your mayoral
race is permitted under the existing injunction, certain other
restrictions of Proposition 73 remain in effect. For example, the
contribution limitation of $1,000 (Section 85301) from any one
person to a candidate in any fiscal year has not changed not-
withstanding any transfer(s) of funds to another controlled com-
mittee. Thus, if contributor "A" gives $500 to your council com-
mittee, and you immediately transfer that contribution, you would
not be able to accept more than an additional $500 from
contributor "A" in the same fiscal year that the first contribu-
tion was made.

Our File No. A-90-432
Page 3

I hope this response answers your question. If you have any
further questions regarding the proposed transfer of campaign
funds, please do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Susan L. Bobrow
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:plh

Enclosures
